1: Nadab and Abihu offered esh zarah (ʾēš zārāh): “unauthorized/strange/foreign fire” — zārāh means strange, foreign, not belonging; the fire they offered was not the fire from YHWH's altar (16:12 specifies that the high priest must take coals from the altar before YHWH). Their error may have been the fire's origin, the incense formula, the timing, or acting outside their priestly brief — the text does not specify; what matters is the phrase “which he had not commanded them” (ʾăšer lōʾ ṣiwwāh ʾōtām). Unauthorized worship, however sincere or beautiful, is not acceptable to the holy God.
2: Fire from before YHWH consumed Nadab and Abihu — the same ʾēš (fire) that accepted the inaugural offering in 9:24 destroys the priests who violated the holy; the divine consuming fire is not primarily a tool of judgment but an expression of YHWH's holiness that both blesses obedience and destroys transgression. Hebrews 12:29: “our God is a consuming fire” — the NT draws on this episode to ground the call to reverent, acceptable worship.
3: “Biqrovai ekadesh” (biqrōbay ʾeqqādēš): “among those who are near me I will be sanctified” — YHWH declares the theological principle Nadab and Abihu violated: proximity to YHWH increases, not decreases, the demand for holiness. Those nearest to the fire feel its full heat. “Before all the people I will be glorified” (wᵉʿal pᵉnê kol hāʿām ʾekkābēd): YHWH's glory is upheld through the deaths of Nadab and Abihu; the people learn the cost of irreverence. 1 Peter 3:15: “sanctify (hagiasate) Christ as Lord in your hearts” — the same qādaš verb applied to the new covenant community's obligation.
4: Moses summoned Mishael and Elzaphan (Aaron's cousins) to carry the bodies outside the camp — the dead priests were removed in their priestly coats; non-ordained relatives handle what the surviving priests (Aaron and sons) may not touch during service. The separation of grief and ministry is painfully enforced; YHWH's service does not pause for human sorrow.
5: Mishael and Elzaphan carried the bodies outside the camp in their coats (bᵉkuttōnōtām) — the priestly vestments become shrouds; the garments that signified consecration and life now wrap death. The bodies are taken to the outside-the-camp location — the place of what is condemned, discarded, and burned. Hebrews 13:13: “let us go to him outside the camp and bear the reproach he endured.”
6: Moses forbids Aaron and his surviving sons to uncover their heads (rōʾšêkem ʾal tipraʿû) or tear their clothes (ubigdêkem lōʾ tiphrōmû), lest they die — the usual mourning rites (loose hair, torn clothes) are prohibited for the priests during their active service; their anointing has created an obligation that overrides natural family grief. The shemen hamishchah (šemen hammiššāḥ) upon them binds them to a higher call than private emotion.
7: “The shemen mishchat YHWH (šemen miššaḥat YHWH) is upon you” — the anointing creates a new identity overriding all prior obligations; the consecrated person belongs to YHWH's service above all else. Romans 12:1: “present your bodies as a living sacrifice” — the new covenant anointing (the Spirit) consecrates the believer to a service that claims all of life.
8: YHWH spoke directly to Aaron — notably, this is one of the rare moments in Leviticus when YHWH addresses Aaron directly rather than through Moses; the command that follows (vv 9-11) is given to the high priest as the direct recipient. After the trauma of his sons' deaths, YHWH speaks to Aaron personally, entrusting him with the principle that explains what happened.
9: “Yayin veshekhar al tishteh” (yayyin wᵉšēkār ʾal tište): “wine and strong drink you shall not drink” — the priest must approach YHWH with faculties unimpaired; šēkār is fermented drink stronger than wine; the prohibition applies specifically when entering the tent of meeting for ministry. Ephesians 5:18: “do not get drunk with wine... but be filled with the Spirit” — the NT's counterpart: the Spirit-filled, not wine-altered, is the proper state for worship; the prohibition of wine is a positive prescription for Spirit-dependence.
10: “To havdil” (lᵉhavdîl): “to distinguish/separate” — between haqodesh (the holy) and hachol (the common), and between hatame (the unclean) and hatahor (the clean). The root bādal (to separate) is the verb of creation itself (Genesis 1:4, 6, 7, 14, 18 — God separates light from dark, waters from waters); the priest perpetuates the creation-work of differentiation, maintaining ordered distinctions within the sanctuary. Hebrews 5:14: “mature believers trained by constant practice to distinguish (diakrínein) good from evil” — the priestly discernment function extends to the new covenant community.
11: The priests are to teach Israel all the statutes (ulᵉhôrôt ʾet bᵉnê yiśrāʾēl ʾēt kol haḥuqqîm) — the priests' teaching function is inseparable from their ritual function; those who maintain the distinctions (v10) also explain and transmit them. Jeremiah 31:34 promises that in the new covenant “they shall all know me, from the least of them to the greatest” — the new covenant distributes the priestly teaching function to all; Christ is the one Teacher (Matthew 23:10) through whom all the tôrāh is rightly understood.
12: Moses addressed “the remaining ones” (hanotar'im, hānôtārîm) — Eleazar and Ithamar; the Levitical line narrows from four sons to two in a single day; the text marks the reduction without softening it. The priestly succession continues despite the loss; YHWH's program of priestly mediation is not derailed by human failure.
13: “Eat it in a holy place (māqôm qādôš), for it is your choq” (ḥōq, statutory due) — the priests' food from YHWH's fire offerings is their permanent statutory portion (ḥōq from the root of “inscribed/decreed”); it is written into the law's permanent structure. The eating is a priestly act of service, not merely sustenance; receiving the holy food is itself a sacred act.
14: The wave breast (ḥāzeh hattᵉnûpāh) and the heave thigh may be eaten in a makom tahor (māqôm ṭāhôr), a clean (but not necessarily holy) place — the degree of sanctity of the food determines where it may be eaten; the most holy portions require the holy place; the less restricted portions require only a clean place. The entire priestly food system involves graduated holiness, matching the tabernacle's spatial gradations.
15: The right thigh and wave breast are given as the priests' permanent portion to wave before YHWH — the tᵉrûmāh (contribution/heave offering; from rûm, to lift/elevate) and the tᵉnûpāh (wave offering) together constitute the priests' statutory share. The offering is first presented to YHWH and received back — the pattern of divine gift through human mediation that characterizes the entire sacrificial system.
16: Moses learned the sin-offering goat had been burned instead of eaten — he “diligently inquired” (dārōš dāraš, intensive form: thoroughly sought); Moses's careful oversight of every sacrificial detail reflects the seriousness with which YHWH's prescriptions are maintained. The doubled verb (dārōš dāraš) signals intensive action: Moses didn't casually notice but actively investigated.
17: “Why have you not eaten the sin offering in the place of the sanctuary?” — Moses's rebuke establishes the principle: eating the sin offering (ʾăkal ʾet haḥaṭṭāʾt) is part of the atonement act itself; the priests bear the congregation's iniquity (yiśśᵉʾû ʾet ʿăwōn hāʿēdāh) by eating it. The priestly eating of the sin offering is a vicarious act — the priest absorbs the sin symbolically. Christ as the ultimate sin-bearer: “he himself bore our sins in his body on the tree” (1 Peter 2:24).
18: Since the goat's blood was not brought inside the sanctuary, it should have been eaten by the priests in the sanctuary — the distinction that determines eating vs. burning: blood brought inside (most holy place) = carcass burned outside; blood not brought inside = carcass eaten by priests inside. The blood's trajectory determines the flesh's disposition; atonement ritual logic is precise and internally consistent.
19: Aaron's explanation: “Such things as these have happened to me” — on the day his two sons were killed, eating the sin offering in a state of grief would have been improper; Aaron invokes the theological principle that the worshiper's heart-state affects the acceptability of the ritual act. The law here accommodates the weight of human grief rather than crushing it; the pastoral wisdom beneath the legal prescription.
20: “Moses heard, and vayitav be'einav” (wayyîṭab bᵉʿênāyw): “it was good in his eyes” — Moses accepted Aaron's explanation; the legal framework shows flexibility in extraordinary human circumstances. The law is not a mechanical system but a relational covenant that accommodates human reality. Hebrews 4:15: Christ as our high priest “is able to sympathize with our weaknesses” — the sympathetic high priest exercises pastoral wisdom rather than rigid legal application, fulfilling what Moses modeled here.
